div.div: · Chinese rings apparatus: 7-9 rings on a bar with looped handle

The Chinese Rings has a series of rings, each linked through the next, all threaded on a bar. To remove a ring, the ring directly behind it must currently be on the bar, and all rings in front must be off. Work one ring at a time. The optimal solution for 9 rings requires exactly 341 moves — there's no shortcut. The puzzle is mathematically equivalent to counting in binary. A beginner should start with 5 or 6 rings to learn the pattern before attempting the full set.

The Chinese Rings puzzle appears in European mathematical literature as early as 1550, when Girolamo Cardano described it. It is mathematically isomorphic to the binary number system — each ring position corresponds to a binary digit — a connection not formally proved until the 20th century.

A series of rings are looped onto a bar with a handle. Each ring passes through the next, and all must be removed from the bar following a precise sequence. To remove ring n, ring (n-1) must be on the bar and all preceding rings must be off. The minimum number of moves for nine rings is 341.

svg.text: Source: Puzzles Old and New (1893) · Heritage Parlor
